directions.name: Restore the accepted-correct control flow and switch the 64x64 column sweep to explicit right-left-right-left order
directions.hypothesis: Three rounds proved that the peeled steady-state control idea is fast but not quickly repairable. The next high-signal move is to separate correctness from warp scheduling again: restore the proven generic hot-band loop, keep the shared PTX hot-band family, and apply the user's warp-local register-reuse idea directly by changing the 64x64 B-column sweep from the current left-right mirrored order to an explicit right-left-right-left order. That gives one clean experiment on the accepted-correct surface and tells us whether warp-local operand order is the real remaining headroom.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Per-warp operand delivery and register reuse inside the 64x64 PTX hot-band microkernel rather than CTA-level pipeline control.
directions.risk: Moderate. The control-flow restore is straightforward, and the new behavior is isolated to warp-local fragment order on the proven correct surface.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if the restored control flow is correct but the right-left-right-left sweep leaves hot-band time flat or regresses relative to the accepted-correct surface.

directions.name: Keep the accepted-correct loop and deepen warp-local Ps2r with next-A-row-pair lookahead
directions.hypothesis: If a simple right-left-right-left sweep is too weak, the stronger human Ps2r branch is to prefetch more than the next B fragment. The current PTX microkernel already overlaps one B-fragment load with the current MMA pair, but A row-pair loading still serializes. A next-A-row-pair lookahead on the accepted-correct loop would test whether the remaining gap is really the A-side fragment supply cadence.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local shared-to-register latency on the A-side of the 64x64 PTX microkernel.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. This can easily increase live fragments and damage the current register surface.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if register pressure rises or if flatter short-scoreboard does not convert into a main-kernel runtime win.

directions.name: Return to a light L2-friendly logical CTA swizzle once correctness is back on the accepted surface
directions.hypothesis: The L2 branch is still relevant, but it should not be mixed with a known-incorrect control-flow experiment. Once the kernel is back on the accepted-correct surface, a light logical CTA swizzle remains the cleanest cache-locality test across the 60x25 hot-band grid.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA L2 reuse across neighboring hot-band B tiles.
directions.risk: Moderate. Previous grouped traversal regressed, so this stays a lower-ranked cache-locality branch.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if L2 throughput stays flat or if runtime regresses like the earlier grouped traversal.

notes: Round 7/50 pivots away from peeled control-flow experiments because the main hot-band speedup is real but correctness has not recovered after multiple repair attempts. The recommended next move is therefore dir_01: restore the accepted-correct hot-band loop and test the user's warp-local right-left-right-left register-reuse idea as an isolated experiment.
